text: Since they can make this guarantee, they can largely use immutable references without giving up performance. And it's clear to the programmer where any cycles could be: in the mutable references. Very cool paper!

elements.text: Since they can make this guarantee, they can largely use immutable references without giving up performance. And it's clear to the programmer where any cycles could be: in the mutable references. Very cool paper!

text:
If the associations are using paper then I think there is a gap that needs to be bridged before even getting to no-code solutions. Their data isn't currently inside a computer system, and they need a change to their working practices before it is.

In my experience, "top down" digital transformations are not popular with practitioners, because the vast majority of the cost of the transformation is borne by them, and the vast majority of the benefit is realised by others. It might be the case that paper workflows actually work well for the associations, in some sense, even if they are nightmare for the auditing non-profit.

I would focus on solutions that meet the people where they are. Humans shouldn't have to move to accommodate computers.

In practical terms, I would buy all the associations sheet-fed scanners that just pump anything scanned to the central auditors, then run your competitions in the frame of providing solutions that build on that messy data source. For example, they can provide an Elasticsearch front end that lets the associations query back over their data (that has been OCRed and parsed).

In terms of truly non-technical people building apps with the same ease they can currently design paper workflows - no, I don't believe the technology is there yet.

text:
Thank you Chris, that's some very helpful feedback. Especially to be wary of a "top down" approach, perhaps I can temper some of the proposal-idealism by calling a few associations and hearting what their pain points are. I already had one reach out to me (part of what started this), but I don't think my understanding of the pain point is quite complete.

I'm still wondering if there is an opportunity for some kind of hackathon, because these are already operating as volunteer organizations. Since many elements of digital transformation are one-time, it could be a good fit for the time-bound nature of competitions, as long as the incentive structures are developed correctly.

text: I agree with <@UDQBTJ211>, and would add that when you start replacing the current workflows you should probably build up the new systems on top of something quite mundane (say, Google Sheets) with a thin layer of automation rather than starting with loads of large scale software development.

elements.text: , and would add that when you start replacing the current workflows you should probably build up the new systems on top of something quite mundane (say, Google Sheets) with a thin layer of automation rather than starting with loads of large scale software development.

text:
The longest lasting part of tech is the data and the API. Languages change, codebases change, but data and APIs live forever. My two cents, use the grant to make an extendable API to expose all necessary data and actions. Once that's up, then you can definitely make micro-grants to let people use the API to solve their problems.

To make something particularly long lasting, focus on doing everything with standards rather than particular tech, like HTTP, REST, JSON, and SQL.

elements.text:

My two cents, use the grant to make an extendable API to expose all necessary data and actions. Once that's up, then you can definitely make micro-grants to let people use the API to solve their problems.

To make something particularly long lasting, focus on doing everything with standards rather than particular tech, like HTTP, REST, JSON, and SQL.

text:
Thank you for the feedback Jack and tyler. The way I'm seeing it evolve now is perhaps taking an iterative approach to do just that. Round one will be very mundane using off-the-shelf solutions, round two will be focused more on the data and how it interacts, and round three could become more of the focused API creation using the boringest languages. But if the customers are really happy with just round one, I'll leave it open to the possibility of stopping the project there.

I did something similar with my last project where we started with a simple jotform survey, built a more advanced version in bubble, and then created the opportunity for a custom app, But ultimately the market told us that wasn't needed.

text: Tim, you might want to have a look at what Richard Pawson did for the Irish government. See for example <https://www.infoq.com/presentations/Large-scale-Pure-OO-Irish-Government/?itm_source=presentations_about_Naked_Objects&itm_medium=link&itm_campaign=Naked_Objects|https://www.infoq.com/presentations/Large-scale-Pure-OO-Irish-Government/?itm_sourc[…]about_Naked_Objects&itm_medium=link&itm_campaign=Naked_Objects>. It is a completely different way of looking at the automation (you're not automating the process, your automating the tools to allow people to do their work). I'm really a big fan of Richard's work. Don't let the awkward naming or weird user interface fool you. The power is having tools that help people instead of hinder them (because of fixed processes, rigid rules, etc).

attachments.title: Case Study: Large-scale Pure OO at the Irish Government
attachments.text: Richard Pawson discusses a case study of a large pure OO project for the Irish government, presenting the challenges met, the reason for choosing pure OO, and lessons learned implementing it.
attachments.fallback: InfoQ: Case Study: Large-scale Pure OO at the Irish Government

text: This is the memory management strategy I've chosen for Curv, but I didn't think it was new. I thought I was stealing the idea from APL. Nice paper, it lays out the ideas very clearly. <https://aplwiki.com/wiki/Performance#Reference_counting_and_data_reuse>

text: So maybe the PLD community is balkanized. APL has been refcounted since the very beginning in the mid 1960s. APL implementors have been sweating the details on how to make a ref counted functional language fast for 55 years.
